comments.body: CodeAnt AI is reviewing your PR.

Reviewer's guide (collapsed on small PRs) ## Reviewer's Guide Replaces a LINQ-based snapshot of Positions (ToArray) with direct foreach iteration to remove heap allocations while preserving existing matching and early-break behavior, and updates comments to reflect the new zero-allocation approach. #### Flow diagram for zero-allocation position search in ShouldSkipFleet_RunHealthCheck ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start ShouldSkipFleet_RunHealthCheck] --> B[Initialize brokerPos = null] B --> C[Iterate positions with foreach pos in acct.Positions] C --> D{pos != null and pos.Instrument.FullName == Instrument.FullName} D -->|true| E[Set brokerPos = pos] E --> F[break from foreach] D -->|false| C F --> G[Continue ShouldSkipFleet_RunHealthCheck with brokerPos] C -->|no more positions| G ``` ### File-Level Changes | Change | Details | Files | | ------ | ------- | ----- | | Remove Positions.ToArray snapshot and iterate Positions directly to eliminate heap allocation while preserving behavior. |
  • Remove creation of _posSnapshot via acct.Positions.ToArray and associated index-based for-loop.
  • Introduce foreach iteration over acct.Positions to find the matching position.
  • Retain early-break behavior when a matching Instrument.FullName is found.
  • Update performance-related comments to describe direct iteration and zero-allocation behavior.
| `src/V12_002.SIMA.Fleet.cs` |
---
Tips and commands #### Interacting with Sourcery - **Trigger a new review:** Comment `@sourcery-ai review` on the pull request. - **Continue discussions:** Reply directly to Sourcery's review comments. - **Generate a GitHub issue from a review comment:** Ask Sourcery to create an issue from a review comment by replying to it. You can also reply to a review comment with `@sourcery-ai issue` to create an issue from it. - **Generate a pull request title:** Write `@sourcery-ai` anywhere in the pull request title to generate a title at any time. You can also comment `@sourcery-ai title` on the pull request to (re-)generate the title at any time. - **Generate a pull request summary:** Write `@sourcery-ai summary` anywhere in the pull request body to generate a PR summary at any time exactly where you want it. You can also comment `@sourcery-ai summary` on the pull request to (re-)generate the summary at any time. - **Generate reviewer's guide:** Comment `@sourcery-ai guide` on the pull request to (re-)generate the reviewer's guide at any time. - **Resolve all Sourcery comments:** Comment `@sourcery-ai resolve` on the pull request to resolve all Sourcery comments. Useful if you've already addressed all the comments and don't want to see them anymore. - **Dismiss all Sourcery reviews:** Comment `@sourcery-ai dismiss` on the pull request to dismiss all existing Sourcery reviews. Especially useful if you want to start fresh with a new review - don't forget to comment `@sourcery-ai review` to trigger a new review! #### Customizing Your Experience Access your [dashboard](https://app.sourcery.ai) to: - Enable or disable review features such as the Sourcery-generated pull request summary, the reviewer's guide, and others. - Change the review language. - Add, remove or edit custom review instructions. - Adjust other review settings. #### Getting Help - [Contact our support team](mailto:support@sourcery.ai) for questions or feedback. - Visit our [documentation](https://docs.sourcery.ai) for detailed guides and information. - Keep in touch with the Sourcery team by following us on [X/Twitter](https://x.com/SourceryAI), [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/company/sourcery-ai/) or [GitHub](https://github.com/sourcery-ai).

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request replaces the snapshot-based iteration over `acct.Positions` with a direct `foreach` loop to eliminate heap allocations. However, the reviewer points out that this introduces a critical thread-safety risk because the `Positions` collection is updated by the broker thread, which can cause an `InvalidOperationException` during iteration. Reverting to the snapshotting pattern is recommended to ensure stability.

Hey - I've found 1 issue, and left some high level feedback:

- The original comment explicitly mentioned snapshotting to avoid broker-thread mutation during iteration; removing the ToArray() snapshot reintroduces potential concurrent-modification issues and InvalidOperationExceptions unless acct.Positions is guaranteed to be thread-safe for concurrent enumeration, so consider either documenting that guarantee or restoring a safe access pattern (e.g., locking or a different non-allocating snapshot strategy).
- The new comment claims 'zero heap allocations', but foreach over acct.Positions will still allocate an enumerator for most reference-type collections; consider rewording this to describe the concrete improvement (removal of the Position[] allocation) rather than implying no allocations at all.
- The note about preserving O(1) best-case performance is slightly misleading since the early-break on the first matching position already existed in the previous implementation; you may want to clarify that behavior is unchanged and the primary benefit is reduced allocations, not an asymptotic complexity change.

<details>
<summary>Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

~~~markdown
Please address the comments from this code review:

## Overall Comments
- The original comment explicitly mentioned snapshotting to avoid broker-thread mutation during iteration; removing the ToArray() snapshot reintroduces potential concurrent-modification issues and InvalidOperationExceptions unless acct.Positions is guaranteed to be thread-safe for concurrent enumeration, so consider either documenting that guarantee or restoring a safe access pattern (e.g., locking or a different non-allocating snapshot strategy).
- The new comment claims 'zero heap allocations', but foreach over acct.Positions will still allocate an enumerator for most reference-type collections; consider rewording this to describe the concrete improvement (removal of the Position[] allocation) rather than implying no allocations at all.
- The note about preserving O(1) best-case performance is slightly misleading since the early-break on the first matching position already existed in the previous implementation; you may want to clarify that behavior is unchanged and the primary benefit is reduced allocations, not an asymptotic complexity change.

## Individual Comments

### Comment 1
<location path="src/V12_002.SIMA.Fleet.cs" line_range="479-482" />
<code_context>
-                // [939-P0]: Snapshot Positions to prevent broker-thread mutation during iteration.

-                Position[] _posSnapshot = acct.Positions.ToArray();
+                // T-W1-Perf: Direct iteration over Positions collection - zero heap allocations
+                // Early-break on match preserves O(1) best-case performance

-                for (int _pi = 0; _pi < _posSnapshot.Length; _pi++)
+                foreach (var pos in acct.Positions)
                 {
-                    if (_posSnapshot[_pi] != null && _posSnapshot[_pi].Instrument.FullName == Instrument.FullName)
</code_context>
<issue_to_address>
**issue (bug_risk):** Direct iteration over acct.Positions may reintroduce concurrent modification issues that the snapshot protected against.

The previous version explicitly snapshot `acct.Positions` to avoid broker-thread mutations during iteration. Removing that and iterating directly can now cause `InvalidOperationException` or expose inconsistent state if the broker thread still mutates the collection. Unless `Positions` is now guaranteed to be iteration-safe, please either restore a snapshot (possibly via a cheaper mechanism) or clearly enforce/document that safety guarantee.
</issue_to_address>
~~~

</details>

## Summary

This PR claims to eliminate LINQ allocations by removing `.ToArray()` but introduces a **critical concurrency defect** that blocks merge.

### Blocking Issue

**Thread-Safety Violation**: The change removes defensive `.ToArray()` snapshot that protected against broker-thread mutations during iteration. The original code explicitly documented this protection (line 479: "[939-P0]: Snapshot Positions to prevent broker-thread mutation"). Direct iteration over `acct.Positions` risks `InvalidOperationException` crashes when broker events modify the collection concurrentlyÔÇöa common scenario during reconnects or position updates.

### Recommendation

Revert to the thread-safe snapshot approach. The performance gain from eliminating one allocation does not justify production crash risk in a trading system.

---
You can now have the agent implement changes and create commits directly on your pull request's source branch. Simply comment with /q followed by your request in natural language to ask the agent to make changes.

### Pull Request Overview

While this PR achieves its goal of eliminating the heap allocation from `.ToArray()`, it re-introduces a critical thread-safety risk. The removal of the defensive snapshot on `acct.Positions` makes the iteration susceptible to `InvalidOperationException` if the broker thread modifies the collection during the loop. This contradicts the PR's claim of 'identical logic' and 'behavioral identity.' 

Although Codacy reports the PR is 'up to standards' based on static analysis, the functional regression regarding concurrency is a major blocker. You should consider an alternative iteration strategy, such as an index-based `for` loop (if the collection supports it) or explicit locking, to maintain thread safety without the allocation overhead.

#### About this PR
- The PR description claims 'Identical logic' and 'Behavior: Identical,' but removing the protective snapshot deviates from the stability requirements established in previous fixes (e.g., [939-P0]). In a multi-threaded trading environment, removing this protection without an alternative concurrency strategy is a significant risk.



#### Test suggestions
- [ ] Iterate through positions and match instrument without allocating an intermediate array
- [ ] Confirm early-break occurs immediately when the correct instrument is found
- [ ] Verify behavior when acct.Positions is modified concurrently by a broker thread during iteration

<details>
<summary>Prompt proposal for missing tests</summary>

```
Consider implementing these tests if applicable:
1. Iterate through positions and match instrument without allocating an intermediate array
2. Confirm early-break occurs immediately when the correct instrument is found
3. Verify behavior when acct.Positions is modified concurrently by a broker thread during iteration
```

</details>
